title: Needle-felted deer
subtitle: A tawny standing fawn with white spots in needle-felted merino, about 12 cm tall
excerpt: A standing fawn needle-felted from fawn and white merino, approximately 12 cm tall. The four legs are built on a stab-compacted wool armature rather than wire, keeping the form safe to handle and simple to construct. White spots are added at the final stage by surface-needling small tufts of white merino into the back.
sourceNotes: Observation-led from contemporary needle-felting practice. The armature-free four-legged standing form follows the same structural logic taught in Wendy Garrison's Needle Felting From Scratch; the method is synthesised in the author's own prose and not quoted from that text.

glossaryTerms.definition: Coarser, less processed wool used as the internal bulk of a needle-felted form. Core wool compacts quickly and cheaply, building up volume and structure before finer merino is layered over the outside to give a smooth, dense surface. It is not usually visible in the finished piece.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

content.text: A standing fawn in needle-felted merino is one of the more satisfying four-legged figures to make because all four legs carry weight structurally, built from stab-compacted wool rather than wire. The finished deer stands at approximately 12 cm tall, worked in fawn (light brown) merino for the body and white merino for the belly patch and spots.
Core wool

is used to build up the internal bulk of the body and each leg quickly; fawn merino is then layered over the outside to give the surface its colour and density. The white spots go on last, so the surface is fully compacted before any detailing begins.
What you need

items.name: White merino roving (belly patch and spots)
Natural or grey core wool (coarser grade)
High-density foam felting mat, at least 10 cm deep
Palm guard or folded kitchen sponge (to protect the supporting hand)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Build the body base

Take a palm-sized clump of core wool and roll it into a rough oval about 7 cm long and 4 cm wide. This is the body of the deer. Lay it on the foam mat and stab repeatedly with the 36-gauge needle, turning the oval as you work to compact all sides evenly. The core wool will firm up after 5-8 minutes of steady stabbing. The oval should hold its shape when you press it lightly between two fingers without springing back. Do not aim for a smooth surface at this stage; you are building density, not finish.
Step 2: Make and attach the four legs

Roll four equal lengths of core wool into firm cylinders, each approximately 4 cm long and 1 cm in diameter. Stab each cylinder on the mat until it is firm and holds its shape. Position the legs at the four corners of the body oval: the front pair closer to the narrow front end, the rear pair closer to the wider back end. Hold each leg against the body and stab repeatedly through the join point, working from the body side into the leg, to fuse them together. Spend at least 3-4 minutes per leg join. Check that the deer stands flat on the mat before proceeding; adjust the angle of any leg by stabbing on the inside face of the join to push it outward, or the outside face to push it inward.
Step 3: Add the neck and head

Roll a smaller cylinder of core wool for the neck, about 3 cm long and 1.5 cm wide. Stab it until firm, then attach to the front top of the body. For the head, roll a slightly flattened oval of core wool about 2.5 cm long. Compact it and attach to the top of the neck, angling it slightly forward so the muzzle faces downward at a gentle graze angle. A fawn's head is relatively large in proportion to the body; do not reduce the head size too much. Check the profile from the side: the neck rises at a forward angle from the shoulders, and the head extends from the neck with the muzzle tipped slightly down.
Step 4: Cover with fawn merino

Pull thin wisps of fawn merino from the roving and lay them over the body, neck, head, and legs, a small section at a time. Switch to the 38-gauge needle for this stage. Stab each wisp into the core surface, working in overlapping passes to build an even coverage with no bare patches showing through. Pay particular attention to the joins at the leg roots and at the neck-to-head join: add extra merino at these points and stab firmly to smooth out any ridges. Work around the entire figure twice before switching to the 40-gauge needle for a final surface pass to refine the smoothness.
Step 5: Add the belly patch and ears

Pull a small wisp of white merino and press it onto the underside of the body, between the four legs. Stab it in with the 40-gauge needle using short, shallow strokes to avoid pushing the white fibre too deep into the fawn-coloured layer. The belly patch should be oval, roughly 3 cm long. For the ears, pull two small pinches of fawn merino and roll each into a slightly flattened teardrop shape on the mat, stabbing gently until just firm. Attach each ear to the top of the head, one on each side, using the 38-gauge needle to secure them at the base. A fawn's ears are large relative to the head; keep them pointed and upright.
Step 6: Add the white spots

The spots go on only once the body surface is fully compacted and even. Pull very small pinches of white merino, each about the size of a small pea. Place each pinch on the back of the deer and stab it in with the 40-gauge needle using 5-10 short, precise strokes per spot. Do not stab too deep or the spot will disappear below the surface. Aim for 8-12 spots across the back and rump, arranged in two rough rows running from shoulder to hip. Press each spot lightly with a fingernail once needled in: it should feel flush with the surface rather than raised.
Step 7: Final shaping and finishing

Stand the deer on a flat surface and check the silhouette from all four sides. Use the 40-gauge needle to refine any areas that look lumpy or asymmetric: press the problem area gently between two fingers to feel the density, then stab in the sparse patches to even them out. The finished figure should measure approximately 12 cm from hoof to ear tip and stand without support. If any leg refuses to sit flat, place the deer on the foam mat and stab down through the base of the leg at a very slight outward angle until the stance corrects.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The legs wobble or the deer falls over
items.cause: The leg-to-body joins were not compacted enough, or the legs are not all the same length
items.fix: Measure all four legs against each other before attaching. If the deer is already assembled, add more fawn merino at the join point of any loose leg and stab firmly through the join from multiple angles to lock the fibres together.

items.problem: The white spots sink and disappear into the surface
items.cause: The spots were added before the body surface was fully compacted, or the needle strokes were too deep
items.fix: Use a 40-gauge needle and very short strokes when adding spots. If a spot has disappeared, lay a new pinch of white merino in the same position and use 3-4 very shallow strokes to secure the top fibres only.

items.problem: The neck-to-head join is visible as a ridge
items.cause: Not enough merino was added at the join to blend the two components
items.fix: Pull a thin wisp of fawn merino and lay it across the join, then stab in with the 38-gauge needle to blend the ridge smooth. Repeat with two or three layers of thin wisps until the join disappears.

content.text: Care and finishing

To remove loose surface fibres, run a soft brush or lint roller very lightly over the surface once the figure is complete. Do not machine-wash needle-felted figures: the agitation destroys the structure. If the figure picks up dust over time, brush it gently with a dry soft-bristle brush. Store away from direct sunlight to prevent fading of the natural merino colour, and in a sealed container or bag to protect against moth damage. Wool moth larvae can damage a needle-felted figure as readily as a knitted garment.
